content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 18,
1994
Michelle A. Curtis
Assistant City Attorney
City of Bell Gardens
333 South Hope Street, Thirty Eighth Floor
Los Angeles,
CA
90071—1469
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-94-053
Dear Ms. Curtis:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of City of Bell Gardens Mayor Frank Duran regarding his
responsibilities under Section 89001 of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
QUESTION
If the City of Bell Gardens Chamber of Commerce receives
funds from the city to provide advertising and promotional
services, are the Chamber mailings paid for with these funds
subject to the mass mailing prohibitions of Section 89001?
CONCLUSION
Section 89001 does not restrict mailings produced and
distributed solely by private entities,
such as the City of Bell
Gardens Chamber of Commerce.
However, the prohibitions of Section
89001 apply to Chamber mailingsif:
(1)
any of the costs of
distribution are paid for with public moneys or
(2)
the costs of
design, production,
and printing exceeding $50 are paid for with
public moneys,
and the design, production,
or printing is done
with the intent of sending the item other than as permitted by
Regulation 18901.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000
— 18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No. A-94-053
Page
2
FACTS
The City of Bell Gardens Chamber of Commerce will receive,
under contract, approximately $46,750 from the City of Bell
Gardens for Fiscal Year 1993—94.
The Chamber has other funds, but
the funds from the city constitute most of the Chamber moneys.
Under the terms of the contract with the city, the Chamber is
to provide promotional and advertising services on behalf of the
City of Bell Gardens.
Your letter states that the city has no
organized means for conducting such activities.
The specific terms of the contract require the Chamber to
maintain suitable quarters and employment of competent personnel
to accomplish its terms,
which also include publicizing and
exploiting the city’s advantages.
The Chamber has previously published a directory with a
circulation of more than 200.
This year, the Chamber plans to
distribute the directories to businesses and residences throughout
the city.
It is anticipated that the costs associated with
publication of the directory will exceed $50.
There is no
specific provision in the city’s contract with the Chamber
requiring the Chamber to publish an annual directory.
The directory will contain the photographs of the city
councilmembers.
This will include photographs of City of Bell
Gardens Mayor Frank Duran and Councilmember Rosa Hernandez,
who
share a seat on the Board of Directors of the Chamber of Commerce.
ANALYSIS
Mass Mailings
In June 1988 Proposition 73 amended Section 89001 of the Act
to provide:
“No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at
public expense.”
The Commission adopted Regulation 18901 to
clarify which mailings were permissible and which were prohibited
under the Act.
2
Regulation 18901(a)
provides that a mailing is prohibited
only if all of the following apply:
(1)
Any item sent is delivered,
by any means,
to the recipient at his or her residence, place of
employment or business,
or post office box.
For
2
The Commission’s authority to interpret Section 89001 to avoid
the absurd results of its literal application was upheld in
Watson v. Fair Political Practices Corn.
(1990)
217 Cal.App.3d
1059.


Our File No. A-94-053
Page
3
purposes of this subdivision (a) (1), the item
delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item,
such as a videotape, record, or button, or a
written document.
(2)
The item sent either:
(A)
Features an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which produces or
sends the mailing,
or
(B)
Includes the name, offipe,
photograph, or other reference to an elected
officer affiliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing,
and is prepared
or sent in cooperation, consultation,
coordination,
or concert with the elected
officer.
(3) (A)
Any of the costs of distribution is
paid for with public moneys; or
(B)
Costs of design, production,
and
printing exceeding $50.00 are paid with public
moneys,
and the design, production,
or
printing is done with the intent of sending
the item other than as permitted by this
regulation.
(4)
More than two hundred substantially
similar items are sent,
in a single calendar month,
excluding any item sent in response to an
unsolicited request and any item described in
subdivision (b).
Regulation 18901(a).
Thus,
a mass mailing is prohibited if all of the factors set
out in Regulation 18901(a)
are met.
If the factors are not met,
the mailing is not a “mass mailing” for purposes of Section 89001.
1.
Mass Mailings
Regulation 18901 applies only to a “mass mailing” as defined
in the Act.
“Mass mailing” means over two hundred substantially
similar pieces of mail, but does not include mail which is sent in
response to an unsolicited request, letter or other inquiry,
or
any items that fall within the exceptions set forth in Regulation
18901(b).
(Section 82041.4.)
Your letter states that over 200
directories will be distributed to businesses and residences.
Therefore, this requirement is met.


Our File No. A-94-053
Page
4
2.
Delivery
Regulation 18901(a) (1) restricts items that are mailed or
delivered,
by any means, to a person’s home, office, or post
office box.
(Anaya Advice Letter,
No.
A-91-215..)
Therefore, this
factor is met with respect to the copies of the directory which
exceed 200 and which are delivered to a person’s home, office, or
post office box.
3.
Public Moneys
Section 89001 provides that no newsletter or other mass
mailing shall be sent at public expense.
Regulation 18901(a) (3)
specifies that a mass mailing is “sent at public expense” within
the meaning of Section 89001 if either the costs of distribution
are paid for with public moneys,
or more than $50 in public money
are used to pay for the costs of design, production,
and printing,
and the design, production, or printing is done with the intent of
sending the item other than as permitted under the regulation.
We have advised that a mailing sent by a chamber of commerce
is not “sent at public expense” for purposes of Section 89001
merely because the chamber receives some public moneys from a
city, unless the public entity expressly funds the production and
distribution of the mailing sent by the chamber.
(Warner Advice
Letter,
1-88—392; Leslie Advice Letter,
No. A-89-521.)
Therefore, the question your letter raises is whether the city
expressly funds the production and distribution of the directory
under the terms of its contract to provide promotional and
advertising services for the City of Bell Gardens.
Under your facts, the chamber is obligated to act as the
city’s public relations firm.
According to the city’s contract
with the chamber, the city has no organized means for conducting
promotional and advertising activities.
Therefore, the city has
contracted with the Chamber to provide such services, which
presumably could include publication of a directory.
For example,
the contract requires the Chamber to publicize and exploit the
city’s advantages.
-
Therefore,
we conclude that if y of the costs of
distribution are paid for with city funds,
or if the costs of
design, production,
and printing exceeding $50 are paid with
public moneys, publication of the directory will constitute a mass
mailing.
This would include use of staff resources and facilities
publicly funded under the terms of the contract.
However, the Chamber can publish its directory in any manner
it desires so long as the costs associated with the directory are
paid for entirely with Chamber funds not obtained by means of the
contract with the city.
If this is the case,
we suggest that
the Chamber segregate the moneys received from the city from
chamber moneys which will fund the publication of the directory.


Our File No.. A-94—053
Page 5
4.
Inclusion of a Reference to an Elected Officer
If the item features
3 an elected officer, or includes the
name, photograph, or any reference to an elected officer who is
affiliated with the agency that produces or distributes the
mailing, the item is subject to the restrictions of the
regulation.
(Regulation 18901(a) (2).)
An elected official is affiliated with an agency if the
official is a member, officer or employee of the agency or a
subunit
(such as a committee)
of that agency; has supervisory
control over the agency; or appoints any of. the members of the
agency.
(Regulation 18901(c) (1); Riddle Advice Letter,
No.
A—89—096.)
The directory will include a photograph of Mayor Frank Duran.
Therefore, the directory will feature an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which will produce or send the mailing,
if the mailing is sent at public expense.
Consequently, if the
mailing is sent at public expense as discussed above, this last
factor is also met.
I trust this letter addresses your concerns.
If you have any
further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to
contact me at
(916)
322—59Ol.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
qeke
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel,
Legal Division
An elected officer is “featured” when an item mailed includes
the elected officer’s photograph or signature,
or singles out the
elected officer by the manner of display of his or her name or
office in the layout of the document,
such as by headlines,
caption, type size, typeface, or type color.
(Regulation
18901(c) (2).)
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10 per page.

QUESTION: If the City of Bell Gardens Chamber of Commerce receives
funds from the city to provide advertising and promotional
services, are the Chamber mailings paid for with these funds
subject to the mass mailing prohibitions of Section 89001?

CONCLUSION: Section 89001 does not restrict mailings produced and
distributed solely by private entities,
such as the City of Bell
Gardens Chamber of Commerce.
However, the prohibitions of Section
89001 apply to Chamber mailingsif:
(1)
any of the costs of
distribution are paid for with public moneys or
(2)
the costs of
design, production,
and printing exceeding $50 are paid for with
public moneys,
and the design, production,
or printing is done
with the intent of sending the item other than as permitted by
Regulation 18901.
Commission
regulations appear at

18000
— 18954.

Division
